summary: Atwood argues that programming rules, methodologies, and best practices face a fundamental paradox: the developers who most need them are the least likely to seek them out or follow them. He observes that the majority of programmers are 'unreachable' - they don't read blogs, books, or attend conferences, and can only be found through web searches for quick fixes. After surveying decades of software methodologies from structured programming to Agile, Atwood concludes that all of these elaborate systems can be distilled to four core principles: DRY, KISS, YAGNI, and writing actual code. He suggests that mentorship and invisible guidance from lead programmers may be more effective than formal methodologies.
key_points: The programmers who would benefit most from coding guidelines are the least likely to read or follow them - they are fundamentally 'unreachable'
Software methodologies proliferate partly because their proponents make a living consulting and teaching about them
Focusing on process over results is 'deadly' - inexperienced developers get bogged down trying to apply principles they don't understand
All programming methodologies essentially boil down to DRY, KISS, YAGNI, and actually writing code
Invisible guidance from experienced leads is more effective than explicitly mandating principles to junior developers
money_quotes: Throwing a book of rules at a terrible programmer just creates a terrible programmer with a bruise on their head where the book bounced off.
The defining characteristic of the vast majority of these so-called 'vocational' programmers is that they are unreachable.
So trying to follow them changes the focus from result to process. And that's deadly.
If it can't be explained on a single double-spaced sheet of paper, it's a waste of your time.
Remind me again – who, exactly, are we writing these principles, rules, guidelines, and methodologies for?
It's the job of the lead programmer or manager to see that good principles are followed, perhaps by guiding others invisibly, without explicitly mentioning those principles.
All those incredibly detailed rules, guidelines, methodologies, and principles? YAGNI.
key_insight: The elaborate ecosystem of programming methodologies and best practices largely preaches to the converted, while the developers who need help most remain unreachable - suggesting we should focus on simple fundamentals and hands-on mentorship instead.
